one_line_takeaway: A 2026 SLR-based survey (136 references from 972 screened) that unifies machine unlearning into one four-part taxonomy -- traditional (exact/approximate), verification, domain-centric (graph/federated/diffusion/LLM), and privacy-security -- and highlights that unlearning-for-privacy itself opens membership-inference and reconstruction attack surfaces.
key_finding: The survey's synthesized takeaways: (1) exact/split unlearning (e.g., SISA) guarantees equivalence to retraining but trades storage cost and degrades badly when many or high-frequency deletions are requested; approximate unlearning saves compute and storage but must directly estimate a sample's influence and faces three recurring challenges it names as stochasticity of training, incrementality of training, and catastrophe of unlearning (sharp utility loss when deleting large fractions). (2) Verification is under-served: membership-inference/backdoor-based checks (MIB, Athena, EMA) can confirm forgetting of triggered data but struggle to verify benign samples, and failing an MIA is 'necessary but not sufficient' for successful unlearning. (3) Unlearning designed for privacy paradoxically creates new privacy leakage, because the delta between pre- and post-unlearning model outputs is itself an attack surface. (4) Domain-specific settings (graph feature-mixing during propagation, federated no-data-access constraints, generative-model concept re-emergence after benign fine-tuning) each break assumptions of centralized unlearning and need tailored evaluation (forgetting efficacy, utility, robustness, durability).
core_contribution: This is a survey (not a novel method). Its own contribution is a unified, systematic-literature-review-based taxonomy that organizes the machine-unlearning field into four top-level categories and explicitly compares itself against prior surveys. The four categories are: (1) traditional centralized unlearning, split into exact unlearning (a.k.a. fast/split retraining) and approximate unlearning (further split into certified data removal and Bayesian-based unlearning); (2) unlearning verification/evaluation; (3) domain-centric unlearning covering federated, graph, diffusion-model, and LLM unlearning; and (4) privacy and security issues in unlearning (threats, defenses, and unlearning-as-defense applications). The authors position their integration of these previously separate strands into one taxonomy, plus a per-scenario discussion of open questions, as the distinguishing value over five earlier surveys (see Table 1). Care is needed reading this record: nearly all technical detail (SISA, certified removal, EUBO, FedEraser, GraphEraser, EraseDiff, ECO, etc.) belongs to CITED works the survey catalogs, not to the survey's own method.
threat_model: The survey is genuinely a security/privacy paper (arXiv cs.CR; ACM CCS 'Security and privacy'), so it catalogs rather than invents threat models. The central problem it frames is the 'right to be forgotten' (GDPR, PIPEDA, CCPA): a user requests deletion of their data D_e, and the model provider must erase the influence of D_e from a trained model M without full retraining. It compiles two adversarial threat models from the literature: (a) membership-inference and (b) privacy-reconstruction attacks that exploit the OUTPUT DIFFERENCE between the pre-unlearning model theta_A and post-unlearning model theta_U to infer whether a sample was erased or to reconstruct the erased sample (Chen et al.; label-only variant by Lu et al.; reconstruction by Gao et al., Salem et al., Zanella-Beguelin et al.). It also covers unlearning-as-defense (removing poisoned/adversarial/anomalous data). The survey does not conduct its own attacks or defenses.
limitations: As a survey: (a) scope is bounded by the SLR cut-off of 7 July 2024 and a top-venue/CCF filter, so fast-moving LLM and diffusion unlearning work after that date and lower-venue methods are excluded by construction; (b) it aggregates and re-narrates results reported by cited papers rather than running any head-to-head empirical benchmark, so no controlled comparison of methods under one protocol is provided; (c) the taxonomy overlaps (e.g., graph/federated methods reappear under both domain-centric and traditional certified-removal buckets), and several category boundaries (exact vs approximate; verification metric vs attack) are fuzzy; (d) reported numbers are second-hand from heterogeneous experimental setups and are not normalized.

datasets_used: Not an empirical paper -- the following are datasets the survey CATALOGS (Table 5) as commonly used across the unlearning literature, not datasets the authors ran experiments on
Tabular: Adult (48,842), Credit info (284,807), Covtype (581,012), HIGGS (11,000,000)
quantitative_results: SLR pipeline (the survey's own numbers): 972 papers retrieved on 7 July 2024
After post-2020 filter, de-duplication, and removing sub-6-page papers: 261 papers
After top-venue (Google Scholar top publications + CCF) filtering: 103 papers reviewed
Plus 33 supporting-technique references => 136 total references reviewed

Deletion-capacity bound cited from Sekhari et al.: existing DP-based unlearning can delete up to n / d^(1/4) samples while preserving the test-loss performance guarantee (n = trainset size, d = input dimension)
Named core challenges of approximate unlearning: stochasticity of training, incrementality of training, catastrophe of unlearning (all qualitative, no aggregate metric computed by the survey)
baselines_compared: This survey does not benchmark methods; its 'baselines' are the five prior surveys it contrasts itself against in Table 1:
Mahadevan and Mathioudakis 2021 -- certifiable unlearning for linear models (efficiency/effectiveness/certifiability trade-offs)
Nguyen et al. 2022 -- broad survey of formulations, design criteria, removal requests, algorithms
Wang et al. 2023 -- federated unlearning and its privacy threats
Liu et al. 2024 -- survey dedicated to federated unlearning methods/workflows
Liu et al. 2025 -- security-oriented review of threats, attacks, and defenses in machine unlearning
Distinction claimed: this survey unifies traditional + verification + domain-centric + privacy/security into one SLR-based taxonomy rather than focusing on a single sub-area
